Best regards.

conversation.content: Dear Competitors, The following additions/revisions have been made to the International UAV Competition and the current specifications have been published on our website. The following additions have been made to the '8.3.2. Payloads Related Matters' section: The payloads must be painted with the color specified in the missions section. No additional elements, such as markings, stripes, or QR codes, should be added to the payloads. Systems that do not allow direct control of the payload, such as a parachute system, may be added. However, these systems must be the same color as the payload. No electronic components, whether they serve as active control or not, should be added to the payload. The following additions/revisions have been made to the second task section of '10.1.1. International UAV Competition Fixed Wing Category Mission Descriptions': All UAVs must carry two (2) payloads. The image processing during the mission must be carried out from the air without any altitude restrictions. Teams are responsible for proving to the flight referees that they have detected the target using the image processing method via the ground control station. No flight logs are requested from the teams after the flights. Even if the target is hit without image processing, teams do not receive target hit points. Teams can use a single camera integrated into the auxiliary computer they use for image processing. Apart from this, the use of a secondary camera for viewing the area is not allowed. The following additions/revisions have been made to the second task section of '10.2.1. International UAV Competition Rotary Wing Category Mission Descriptions': All UAVs must carry two (2) payloads. First, the payload painted red on the outside is dropped on the blue colored regular hexagonal target with two (2) meters on each side. After that, the payload painted blue on the outside is dropped on the red colored equilateral triangle target with one (1) meter on each side. The image processing during the mission must be carried out from the air without any altitude restrictions. Teams are responsible for proving to the flight referees that they have detected the target using the image processing method via the ground control station. No flight logs are requested from the teams after the flights. Even if the target is hit without image processing, teams do not receive target hit points. Teams can use a single camera integrated into the auxiliary computer they use for image processing. Apart from this, the use of a secondary camera for viewing the area is not allowed. It is important that you read the Rules Booklet in order to learn about our rule changes in 2025 and follow the process. The current rules booklet published on 13.03.2025 is available in the 'Competition Specifications' section on the competition page. title: TEKNOFEST UAV Competition Technical Question Regarding Payload Pickup and Release Process

conversation.author: ahmetber...@gmail.com
conversation.content: Hello, While reviewing the TEKNOFEST UAV competition rules, we want to clarify some points regarding the payload pickup and release process. Is there any restriction on the UAV fully landing during the task? Also, is there any specific regulation or technical detail to be aware of regarding the ARM status during this process? We want to ensure the rules are applied correctly. Thank you for taking the time. Regards.

conversation.author: ihaulus...@gmail.com
conversation.content: Hello, There is no restriction on the UAV briefly touching the ground while performing the task, but if the UAV is DISARMED in this case, the flight will be considered unsuccessful. Teams will not be allowed to perform ARM operations except at the starting line. ARM/DISARM operations must also be possible via the controller. This is to allow immediate DISARM via the controller in case of an emergency to prevent the situation. Have a good day.

conversation.author: betul4...@gmail.com
conversation.content: Hello, good day, The rulebook refers to a cylindrical storage box by this name, but it is not clearly specified whether this box is expected to be fully cylindrical or prismatic. Can you provide details on what is exactly meant by the term 'cylindrical'? Thank you in advance.

conversation.author: ihaulus...@gmail.com
conversation.content: Hello, As stated in the '8.3.4. Matters Regarding the Storage Box' section of the rulebook: - For Fixed Wing UAVs, the storage box must be cylindrical. The dimensions and manufacturing method are left to the teams' discretion. - For Rotary Wing UAVs, the storage box must be rectangular or square prismatic. The dimensions and manufacturing method are left to the teams' discretion. The term 'cylindrical' refers to the box being cylindrical in shape. Have a good day.

conversation.author: prof.abuze...@gmail.com
conversation.content: Hello, the rulebook states: 'The image processing operation during the task must be performed in the air without any altitude limitation. Teams are obliged to prove to the flight judges through the ground control station that they have detected the target with the image processing method. Flight logs will not be requested from teams after the flights are completed. Even if a target is hit without image processing, teams will not receive target hit points.' Since our team does not need to send commands from the ground control station to the UAV during the task, we prefer not to use a ground control station. The pilot's lack of intervention with the controller is already monitored by the pilot judge. In this case, since it is proven that there is no external intervention to the aircraft, does this fulfill the obligation in the rulebook? If this is not compliant with the rulebook, would transmitting only the raw (unprocessed) image to the ground be sufficient, or is it required to show the processed image with target detection frames to the judges? Best regards.

conversation.author: ihaulus...@gmail.com
conversation.content: Hello, regarding your first question, the obligations will not be considered fulfilled. For them to be fulfilled, the image processing data must be shown to our judges. For example, situations where the autopilot randomly sends a command to a point and opens the servo mechanism, reaching the target or completing the task by chance, are not acceptable. To prevent such uncertainties, teams must clearly show the image processing process, and the judges must observe that the target has been processed, providing the necessary information accordingly. Wishing you a good day.
